author: Dr. Lizard
title: Why is narsoplimab ineffective for treating paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria?
answer: Narsoplimab blocks the lectin pathway via MASP-2, but PNH hemolysis is driven by the alternative pathway.
detail: PNH pathophysiology results from uncontrolled alternative pathway activation through continuous C3 tick-over on GPI-deficient cells.
Narsoplimab targets MASP-2, a serine protease essential for lectin pathway activation, which plays minimal role in PNH.
The lectin pathway contributes negligibly to complement-mediated hemolysis in PNH compared to alternative pathway amplification.
Narsoplimab is FDA-approved for transplant-associated thrombotic microangiopathy, not for PNH treatment.
releaseNote: Narsoplimab is ineffective for treating paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria because it targets the lectin pathway, which plays a minimal role in PNH, whereas the alternative pathway drives hemolysis in this condition.
